Lenty Pond

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you ever say ‚Äòit‚Äôs too late‚Äô again, Missy!‚Äù were his parting words, as he kissed Gerda, a few days later, across their iron gate.

It was Thursday now, only two days before the King‚Äôs Barton wedding, and events had moved rapidly since that agitating Sunday. He had cajoled his Pond Cottage friends into allowing Olwen to pay a surreptitious visit to what after all was her paternal home; and the child had fallen in love with Christie to such an extent that the visit had been repeated within forty-eight hours. And this very day Darnley was driving her in, as he came to School, with the idea that she should stay a couple of days under the Malakite roof.

‚ÄúNo one will interfere; it‚Äôs all blown over,‚Äù Wolf had said. ‚ÄúIt would have to be some enemy if any fuss were made. But there won‚Äôt be any fuss. A little gossip, when Christie goes out with her in the street‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ nothing more‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and perhaps not even that!‚Äù

The only opposition to these proceedings came from Jason, who, though he would not confess himself jealous of this new passion in the child, brought forward the darkest suggestions as to the dangerousness of Blacksod as a place for little girls. ‚ÄúThese large towns,‚Äù he had said to them all, speaking as if Blacksod were a second Birmingham, ‚Äúthese large towns are full of disgusting goings-on. These tradesmen think of nothing but their merry little ways. And, of course, if you want Olwen to have her meals with Mr.¬ÝMalakite‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù But to Wolf‚Äôs delighted surprise he had received emphatic support in this enterprise from Mrs.¬ÝOtter. He had, indeed, been quite as astonished at the insight displayed by that timid lady as by her defiance of the protests of her eldest son. ‚ÄúOlwen will only do them good, Jason,‚Äù she had said. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs a special providence over a child like that. She‚Äôll turn that sad little Christie into a different girl.‚Äù

It was just after eight o‚Äôclock when Wolf swung round to wave a final farewell to Gerda. He had begged her to let him have a very early breakfast that morning; for Mrs.¬ÝSolent wanted him to see her teashop with what she called ‚Äúclear decks.‚Äù Everything had always been in confusion, near closing-time, when he came in at the end of the day; but this morning, full of pride that her son should see her shop before her waitresses appeared, Mrs.¬ÝSolent had unlocked and cleaned up the place herself at an incredibly early hour, and was waiting for him there now.

The new teashop was not far from the Grammar School, but it was in a side-street that branched off towards the meadows where the Lunt encircled the town. The town, in fact, melted into the country here even more quickly than it did on the Babylon Hill side or in the direction of Preston Lane. It was a more umbrageous country, at any rate, into which that little side-street led.

Into this quarter of Blacksod, cutting its way through heavy clay hills, diving between tall ferny banks covered with beeches and Scotch firs, following swifter streams than the Lunt, ran the great Exeter Highroad; and it was the tourists from that direction that were now to be waylaid and entertained.

This process had apparently already begun; for when Wolf approached the neat little square building, lying back from the road, with a garden in front of it yellow with daffodils, his feeling was unmistakable that prosperity was in the air. The wind was keen and invigorating this morning, the sky clear; and as he strode up the path between the swaying daffodils, he had a sharp, prophetic sense of his mother‚Äôs future. He saw this little shop moved to one of the main streets of the town. He saw still more of the savings of that enamoured farmer swept into the business! He saw his mother‚Äôs grasp upon life growing more drastic, more daring, more debonair. He saw her power over material things increasing, her strange pride and exultant loneliness keeping pace with her power. ‚ÄúShe‚Äôll leave me far behind,‚Äù he thought. And there swept over him a wave of bitter shame at his own incompetence.

‚ÄúShe‚Äôll be doling out bonuses to Gerda and me,‚Äù he thought. ‚ÄúWe shall be hanging on to her skirts! We shall be a dead weight upon her.‚Äù

Vividly he recalled the discussion that had taken place in the last few days between himself and Gerda on the subject of how to spend Mr.¬ÝUrquhart‚Äôs two hundred pounds. How childish Gerda was, and how reckless he was! The whole thing was ridiculous‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ with their tiny income to think of spending all this on just smartening up their house!

He knocked lightly now at the teashop door and entered without waiting for a response. He was amazed at the neatness and elegance of what he saw.

His mother greeted him in the highest feather. Laughing and jesting, she showed him the kitchen, the scullery, the sanitary arrangements, the furniture. ‚ÄúThe rooms are empty upstairs,‚Äù she said; ‚Äúbut do you know what I‚Äôm going to do? I‚Äôm going to leave Herbert-land, with its dust and its smells, and move over here! I‚Äôm going to use one of my waitresses‚ÅÝ‚ÄîI‚Äôve got two, you know‚ÅÝ‚Äîas a maid. She and I will both sleep up there. There are three rooms. And I‚Äôll have a regular drawing-room. I‚Äôll have the kind of drawing-room I‚Äôve always wanted‚ÅÝ‚Äîdifferent altogether from that old place in town.‚Äù

Mother and son were now seated on two immaculate wicker-chairs. Wolf had not yet dared to light a cigarette; but Mrs.¬ÝSolent, with a quick, radiant gesture, offered him one of her own.

‚ÄúYou won‚Äôt get enough exercise, mother, if you live where you work; and your precious drawing-room will always be full of the smell of cooking.‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, we won‚Äôt think of that!‚Äù she cried, making a stroke in the air with her cigarette as if condemning to annihilation every trick of hostile chance; and as he watched her, he realized for the first time what a power she had of forcing external events into line with her wishes. Never had he seen her so full of zest for labour and trouble and tension as she was that morning. Wolf himself felt sick with dismay when he thought of this place filled with tourists from Exeter, and the rooms upstairs reeking of culinary odours!

‚ÄúWhat are you making that face about?‚Äù his mother asked.

‚ÄúAm I making a face? I was wondering how much spirit you‚Äôll have left for those evening walks you‚Äôre so fond of.‚Äù

Mrs.¬ÝSolent laughed gaily. ‚ÄúI had one, last night,‚Äù she said, ‚Äútowards Pendomer. There are lovely fields over there‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Äîshe nodded her head towards the west‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äúand delicious woods. I couldn‚Äôt want anything nicer. I went out there last night‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ up the hill and over the hill‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I half-thought of waylaying you at the Grammar School and taking you with me. But you know what I am! I love my Wolf.‚Äù Here she extinguished her cigarette and rose from her seat. ‚ÄúBut I have to be alone for these walks. I tell myself stories; I let myself be as romantic and excited as I can. That time of twilight stirs me up‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ like a nightjar, I suppose‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and I have lovely sensations!‚Äù

She moved past him; and as she passed she bent down, took his head between her two hands, and kissed it. Then she went to the door, and, flinging it wide open, inhaled the cool, strong northeast wind. As she stood thus, with her straight, sturdy back turned to him, he seemed to get a supernatural glimpse of the whole power of her personality. This teashop and that hill ‚Äútowards Pendomer‚Äù were only little, material symbols of a Napoleonic campaign that she was working out‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ not necessarily in this world at all, but in some world, some level of psychic conflict, parallel with his ‚Äúmythology.‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôve got to be off, Mother!‚Äù he cried. And as he extinguished his cigarette by the edge of hers, in one of her new ashtrays, he instinctively squeezed it into an identical perpendicular position. Then, jumping up from the creaking wicker-chair, ‚ÄúI‚Äôm late as it is,‚Äù he murmured. ‚ÄúI suppose Mr.¬Ý‚ÄòWillum‚Äôs Mill‚Äô comes here for his tea every day?‚Äù He strode to the door and stood there by her side. Mrs.¬ÝSolent laughed, with the rich, careless, high-pitched laugh of a Ninon or a Thais.

‚ÄúOnly on market-days, my son. But I‚Äôm going to tea with him, next Sunday.‚Äù

Wolf disregarded this confession altogether. ‚ÄúI say, Mother! You‚Äôre coming to the wedding on Saturday, aren‚Äôt you? The day after tomorrow. You haven‚Äôt forgotten that?‚Äù

She turned towards him her radiant cheeks and glowing eyes. ‚ÄúWill the monster be there, to see Lorna‚Äôs child married to your respectable friend? If she‚Äôs there, I must come. What sport it‚Äôll be! The monster and I in the same pew, and your sister landing her patient fish!‚Äù

Across Wolf‚Äôs mind flitted the image of that unwieldy figure stumbling over the milk-bottle at the grave.

‚ÄúI haven‚Äôt the least idea whether Miss Gault means to come,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúBut you must come, Mother! You must leave your work to your two girls. I‚Äôll call for you at Mrs.¬ÝHerbert‚Äôs‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ about half-past nine‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and we‚Äôll walk over. Well, I must run. Goodbye, Mother.‚Äù

She met his embrace with a swift, almost greedy kiss, but immediately afterwards whispered with airy mockery: ‚ÄúMattie Smith must be very grateful to you for giving her her darling! That pointed beard would never have been caught if my Wolf hadn‚Äôt played matchmaker.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat the devil are you talking about, Mother? They knew each other years before we came down here.‚Äù

There shone in her brown eyes such a wellspring of satirical mischief, he found it hard to tear himself away. A spasm of vicious sympathy with this dark-spurting jet of malice produced the sensation within him of a nervous twinge that was half a tickling delight to him and half an adder‚Äôs bite.

His mind reverted in a lightning-flash to his father‚Äôs skull. Oh, how gentle, oh, how kindly that grin of death seemed, compared with this inhuman glee in the presence of perverse fate! A malign voluptuousness rose up within him, like an intoxicating bubble out of the very abyss, spilling black bile through veins. Ferociously he offered up that poor skull to this radiant sorceress. ‚ÄúYou look just as you did, Mother, when you teased Mr.¬ÝSmith so much, that Horse-Fair day. I hope his ghost won‚Äôt be there on Saturday!‚Äù His words were innocent enough; but he knew too well what passed, under their cover, between himself and this woman. For good and evil he had made his choice between the living and the dead.

‚ÄúI could not feel like this,‚Äù he thought, ‚Äúif I were the Wolf Solent I used to be. Goodbye!‚Äù he repeated. ‚ÄúI must run.‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

All that morning, as he faced the Grammar School boys, his mind squeezed out the essence of this scene with his mother. He had gone over to her altogether! He had deserted the ‚Äúfellow i‚Äô the cellarage.‚Äù He had betrayed his ‚Äúold Truepenny.‚Äù All that long morning, while those boys‚Äô faces scattered themselves into his mind like grey ashes into a pail, he struggled to make clear what had happened to him.

He had no longer any definite personality, no longer any banked-up integral self. Submission to Urquhart had killed more than self-respect. He could never have gone over to his mother like this if his ‚Äúmythology‚Äù had survived. He could feel now that greedy kiss of hers upon his lips! He had come to Dorset‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ he knew it well enough now‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to escape from her, to mix with the spirit of his father in his own land. But Fate was hunting him ‚Äúback to London,‚Äù and he began to have an inkling as to what the alternative to London was. The alternative to London was the bottom of Lenty Pond!

Wilder and wilder grew his thoughts as he rounded off the destiny of the House of Stuart to those furtive listeners. Rows upon rows of dwarf-men‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that is how he saw them now, these boys of his‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ embryo-men, with a kind of distorted, atrophied intelligence, full of a jeering, idiotic cunning! Oh, how he hated them and the task of teaching them!

Suddenly in the very middle of his lesson he felt his voice changing and becoming strangely vibrant. Good God! What things were on the tip of his tongue to say to them! Was he going to ‚Äúdance his malice-dance‚Äù before them, as he had danced it before that London audience? Life upon this earth began to show itself to him in a most evil light.

This killing of his ‚Äúmythology,‚Äù how could he survive it? His ‚Äúmythology‚Äù had been his escape from life, his escape into a world where machinery could not reach him, his escape into a deep, green, lovely world, where thoughts unfolded themselves like large, beautiful leaves, growing out of fathoms of blue-green water!

What were his sensations to him now? What was the air of a morning like this, without those mysterious emanations from the glimmering depths?

He had comforted Gerda; and the way she was happy now in her childish delight over that two hundred ought to have given a fresh glow to his days. But it didn‚Äôt. That startling alliance between Christie and Olwen, which he had plotted in the face of so many difficulties, and which was apparently absorbing both of them in its excitement, ought to have satisfied him. But it only made his thoughts gloomier. The last time he had seen Christie, her mind was so full of Olwen and Olwen‚Äôs future, that she scarcely listened to what he was telling her!

Through the dizzy foreground of these boys‚Äô heads, white collars, sharp elbows, and scratching pens, through the patient ‚Äúnotes‚Äù he himself was dictating to them, floated in long procession all the people of his life.

Urquhart was sending his book in instalments to Bristol to be printed. He appeared to be thinking of nothing else. Jason was revising another volume of poetry, which promised to raise him into the innermost circle of modern literature. Darnley, Mattie, Mrs.¬ÝOtter‚ÅÝ‚Äîthey were all happy just then. He found himself sheering off any thought of Miss Gault. But apart from Miss Gault, all his friends were in calm waters. Even T. E. Valley, so Darnley had informed him, was in a state of comparative peace of mind.

He found himself and Miss Gault to be the only unhappy ones. Yes, and they were the only consciousnesses in the whole circle who gave a thought to that cemetery! When he and Miss Gault were dead, not a living soul would remember William Solent. Why, Mattie, the man‚Äôs own daughter‚ÅÝ‚Äînot even once had Wolf been able to persuade her to visit that grave!

Oh, how he hated his work in that classroom! He did not only know in pitiless detail every map upon the wall‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and feel toward it as something removed from every tincture of happiness‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ he also knew every ink-stain and fly-stain upon the wall. Those dirty marks were of equal importance with the maps. Both the marks and the maps represented a world that was totally bleak‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ a world of doleful invention, of disconsolate fancy‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and yet a world in which he had to spend by far the larger part of his life.

He had just managed to cope with this desolate world by giving himself up to his secret vice the very second he left the school-gate. But those ecstatic sensations were now gone forever! He might tear his nerves to pieces with his effort to get those feelings back. They would never come back! They were lost. How did human beings go on living, when their life-illusion was destroyed? What did they tinker up and patch up inside of them to rub along with, to shuffle through life with, when they lacked that one grand resource?‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

He hurried back to Preston Lane for lunch, and was more than successful in hoodwinking Gerda as to his secret desolation. The girl chatted all the time about the spending of the two-hundred! So far she had bought nothing but one small pair of silver sugar-tongs. The cheque had been deposited in Gerda‚Äôs name, and the girl was touchingly proud of possessing her first ‚Äúfortune,‚Äù as she kept calling it. She apparently intended spending every penny in the next few weeks! At least that was the implication of her excited chatter; and Wolf was quite prepared to submit.

He derived a sardonic amusement from noting the fact that this ‚Äúspiritual blood-money,‚Äù which had cost him his secretest happiness, was apparently smoothing away altogether the moral bruise left by the Weevil incident. That ‚Äúbrown coat‚Äù might return to his mind now and then. Hers it seemingly troubled no more. As for the luckless water-rat, he did not show his face again. Wolf‚Äôs private inkling was that he had been indignantly dismissed, once for all, in some brief scene to which the girl never alluded. But it may easily have been that the lad himself was frightened by the length to which he had gone. Wolf certainly found, in his own weary introspection into the feelings of a cuckold, that he had a tendency to avoid that part of the town where the sausage-shop was!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

His lunch over, Wolf strode back more dispirited than ever to the scene of his pillory.

He had come to loathe every aspect of that chair and desk which made up his spiritual scaffold. There he talked and fidgeted while those rows of cropped heads and protruding ears nodded and swayed like shocks of ruffled wheat under the conscientious, pitiless repetition of a recurrent winnowing. And this was destined to be his life indefinitely, sans the remotest chance of a change for the better, unless his mother, as a successful businesswoman, gave him a pension!

What a mess he had made of his life! As he surveyed those spots and blurs and marks on these odious walls, he began to recognize the fact that until the last two or three days he had never faced reality at all. His heavenly vice, hugged to himself like a fairy bride, had protected him from reality. Here he was, thirty-six years old, and as far as real reality was concerned‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe reality his mother lived in, the reality Darnley lived in‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe was as innocent and preoccupied as a hermit who reads nothing but his breviary.

He had lost his breviary now, his Mass-book, his Mass! He had lost his whole inner world; and the outer world‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhat was it but rows of puzzled, protruding ears, into which, for an eternity, he had to pump tedious, questionable information?

When he left the classroom that evening, he waited for Darnley outside the building.

‚ÄúI must see Christie first!‚Äù he kept repeating, as he watched the boys file out.

‚ÄúWill you do something for me, old friend?‚Äù he said, as soon as his colleague appeared. Darnley fixed his mackerel-coloured eyes upon him in patient surprise.

‚ÄúEven unto the third part of my kingdom, Solent!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, keep Mattie waiting for once, and go to tea with Gerda. Will you do that? Tell her I‚Äôve got one of my walking-fits upon me and have to have some air. Tell her not to be worried, even if I‚Äôm late for supper. Of course, I don‚Äôt mean you to stay all that time. But just tell her I shall be late; and she‚Äôs not to worry.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut what on earth‚Äôs up, Wolf? Where, if a person may ask, are you going to run off to?‚Äù

‚ÄúOh, it‚Äôs all right,‚Äù Wolf said quietly. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm not sure yet where I shall go. Possibly I‚Äôll pay a visit to Mattie and tell her to expect you! Don‚Äôt bother me with any details, my dear! Only, if you love me, go over to Preston Lane and make yourself amusing to Gerda and enjoy her tea. And make her understand that it‚Äôs all right. That‚Äôs the great thing‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ that it‚Äôs all right!‚Äù

Wolf fancied there was a dim expression of disquietude in his friend‚Äôs face as he nodded to him and hurried off; but he felt as if he would have run a worse risk just then than to disquiet Darnley. Hurriedly he made his way to the Malakite shop. ‚ÄúShe‚Äôs got Olwen in there now,‚Äù he said to himself. ‚ÄúShe won‚Äôt want to see me.‚Äù

But while he still kept repeating the words, ‚ÄúShe won‚Äôt want to see me,‚Äù he rang the bell in the little side-alley.

To his surprise the door was opened immediately, and Christie herself, in cloak and hat, stood before him. ‚ÄúYou!‚Äù the girl cried. ‚ÄúWell, you‚Äôd better come with me! Olwen has begun murmuring something about cake; and I‚Äôve got none in the house. I‚Äôve left her with Father, over their tea. They‚Äôre both slow eaters; so we needn‚Äôt rush too madly. Let‚Äôs go this way!‚Äù

She led him up the quiet incline leading to the King‚Äôs Barton road. He could guess now which was the actual confectioner‚Äôs to which she was hurrying him‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ a little shop he had often passed on his way in and out of the town.

The horizontal sun was shooting its rays through great dark banks of western clouds as they approached this shop; and from its windows the fiery reflections fell upon the road like the reflections of barge-lanterns into an estuary.

‚ÄúWolf! I never knew how exciting she was, how intelligent she was! Oh, Wolf, it‚Äôs wonderful! We suit each other down to the ground.‚Äù

He snatched at her hand and pressed it hard. Never in all his relations with her had he caught such a tone in her voice.

When they turned into the Barton road, there was wafted into their faces one of those wandering winds that seem to carry a burden of earth-mysteries from one unknown spot to another.

‚ÄúWhat an evening it is!‚Äù she cried. ‚ÄúI smelt primroses then!‚Äù

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs moss, I expect, and dead leaves,‚Äù he said, ‚Äúfrom the woods over there.‚Äù

They soon reached the little shop; and he entered it with her, and helped her to choose the cake.

‚ÄúWhere are you going, Wolf? Over to Barton? Over to Pond Cottage?‚Äù

He held open the door for her in silence. There was a bell fixed upon the top of this door, which rang noisily at he closed it behind them. His nerves were so strained that this harsh jangle above their heads seemed ominous to him‚ÅÝ‚Äîseemed to have a sound of warning, like a reef-bell at sea.

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù he said dreamily. ‚ÄúOver to Barton‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ over to Lenty Pond.‚Äù

The girl missed this slip of the tongue.

‚ÄúIs your sister happy, Wolf?‚Äù she asked. And then, without waiting for an answer: ‚ÄúDo you know what Olwen said just now? She said she‚Äôd like to live with me when Mattie was married!‚Äù

Wolf prodded the ground with his stick. ‚ÄúDid she really? What a wise little girl! And what did you answer? I don‚Äôt see why you shouldn‚Äôt have her! I‚Äôm sure it would be all right now.‚Äù

Christie sighed deeply, a long breath.

‚ÄúWould they agree to it? Do you think they‚Äôd agree to it?‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt see why not,‚Äù he repeated, in a low voice.

‚ÄúIf you see Mattie tonight, Wolf, I wish you‚Äôd sound her about it‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ and Mrs.¬ÝOtter‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ just to see how they‚Äôd take it.‚Äù

He made no reply to this; but drawing under his arm her free hand, and straightening his shoulders, he gazed up the road.

‚ÄúDo you remember our night in the cornfield, Chris? After that game of bowls?‚Äù

She lifted her head and looked sharply at him, and he received the impression that he had struck an unseasonable note.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm not one for forgetting, Wolf. You ought to know that by this time.‚Äù

‚ÄúUrquhart gave me two hundred pounds for finishing his book, Chris. I‚Äôve never told you that, have I?‚Äù

But she had turned her face away now and was evidently thinking about Olwen, and getting anxious to return.

‚ÄúOh, I‚Äôm so glad, my dear!‚Äù Her voice was sympathetic, but it was the calm sympathy of a friend, not the vibrant sympathy of a lover.

‚ÄúWhat a detached little thing she is,‚Äù he thought; and the memory came over him, with a rush of wild self-pity, of all they had whispered together in that cornfield. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve never told her about my ‚Äòmythology‚Äô‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ but she ought to know, she ought to know what that two hundred means!‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I must run back. Olwen will have finished her tea.‚Äù And she tightened her hold upon the cake and made a little movement to draw her arm away. But Wolf burst out then with a final impulse of desperation:

‚ÄúIt was a vile job. It‚Äôs a vile production to be paid all that for! He‚Äôs printing it in Bristol now. It‚Äôll just suit your father‚Äôs clients! How do you think I‚Äôll appear to myself after this, Christie?‚Äù

The girl tossed her head proudly. ‚ÄúOh, the clients!‚Äù she cried. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôre extremely moral tonight, Wolf! I daresay you thought my book would please the clients!‚Äù

‚ÄúI read just a page,‚Äù he said. But he released her arm now and only held her there by the grimness of his mood. ‚ÄúTo sell my soul to Urquhart!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ to do what young Redfern wouldn‚Äôt do!‚Äù

She did look up at him now with a flash of penetration.

‚ÄúBut, Wolf‚ÅÝ‚Äîany deviltry he threatened you with, was to make you do it, wasn‚Äôt it? Well! You‚Äôve done it. You‚Äôve submitted. He can‚Äôt hurt you now, can he?‚Äù

‚ÄúBut the book‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe book, Chris!‚Äù

The girl gave a faint little laugh‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ the laugh of an air-sprite for whom these human scruples were growing intolerably tedious.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúWell, there are plenty of things Gerda will be glad enough to buy with this money. You‚Äôre different from what I thought you were, Wolf, if you let an absurd fancy like this prey on your mind!‚Äù She paused a moment and then said gravely, ‚ÄúBut Mother would have understood what troubles you.‚Äù

She seized the sleeve of his coat with her fingers, and then stood silent, looking fixedly at him. Then she sighed very heavily, and, lifting up his arm to her face, pressed her lips to his wrist. After that she stared at him once more, in intense contemplative scrutiny.

He looked away, across her shoulder, over the scattered Blacksod roofs, over the Lunt meadows. Her sudden gesture of affection and something in the white immobility of her face made him think of the warning he had received in Urquhart‚Äôs kitchen from that Farmer‚Äôs Rest girl.

‚ÄúI‚Äôll take a look at that pond tonight,‚Äù he thought grimly. ‚ÄúIf that‚Äôs to be the upshot, I‚Äôd better see how it looks of a fine March evening!‚Äù‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶

‚ÄúWell, give Olwen a kiss for me, Chris; and if I find Mattie at home I‚Äôll certainly try her out about that. I believe myself that she‚Äôll agree to it. She‚Äôs so self-absorbed just now that I think she‚Äôll be glad to be left free. Well‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ God bless you, Chris! Don‚Äôt drop the cake. Goodbye‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ goodbye!‚Äù
